the knowledge of ancient Sastras.
Varaha had several predecessors in the field of Samhita, whom
he mentions by name, viz., Garga, Parasara, Ka^yapa, Rsiputra,
Dcvala, Brhaspati, Nandisvara, etc. He mentions both Vrddha
Garga and Garga. He shows great regard for ancient sages. In
every chapter he invariably mentions the authorities on the topic to
be discussed.1 Whenever he differs from their views, he gives his
1. Cf. XXI. 2; XXXII. 1; XLVI. 1.

xvi Brhat Sarhhitd.
own reasons for it.1 His knowledge is really encyclopaedic. For
x
like the Mahabharata there is no subject that he has not discussed in
his magnum opus. The subjects may broadly be classified under the
following heads : (1) Astronomy, (2) Geography, (3) Calendar,
(4) Meteorology, (5) Flora, (6) Portents, (7) Agriculture and Econo-
mics, (8) Politics, (9) Physiognomy, (10) Engineering, (11) Botany,
(12) Industries, (13) Zoology, (14) Erotics, (15) Gemology,
(16) Hygiene, (17) Augurus and (18) Stellar lore. In the first
